<findings>

[observation] [warning] Bidirectional imports with web-dashboard-core (2 each direction) create implicit coupling that undermines the separation this zone implies; consider whether these services should be absorbed into the core zone.
[observation] [info] Clean 1:1 source-to-test pairing (2 src files, 2 test files) indicates good test discipline for this subsystem.
[pattern] [warning] Coupling-to-cohesion ratio of ~0.60 is the highest in this batch — external pull on this zone is nearly as strong as its internal coherence, signaling an unstable boundary.
[anti-pattern] [warning] usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts documents that it 'Broadcasts a WebSocket event so the dashboard can react' but does not import websocket infrastructure directly — the WebSocket dependency is injected at the call site, hiding a runtime coupling that the static zone graph cannot measure. This invisible dependency means the zone's coupling score of 0.38 understates its true runtime coupling.
[suggestion] [warning] Split task-usage-tracking into two single-responsibility units: a domain zone for incremental-task-usage.ts (task event accumulation) and absorb usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts into web-dashboard-core as an operational infrastructure concern. This resolves the layer violation and eliminates the zone's weak cohesion score.

</findings>

<insights>

- The zone consists of exactly two service files and their paired unit tests — a clean, focused scope that makes the zone easy to reason about.
- Bidirectional coupling with web-dashboard-core (web-viewer imports 2, this zone imports 2) suggests these services are tightly integrated with the broader dashboard; evaluate whether they belong inside web-dashboard-core instead.
- Cohesion of 0.63 is acceptable but on the lower end for a 4-file zone — the scheduler and incremental tracker have different lifecycles and could drift apart.
- Bidirectional imports with web-dashboard-core (2 each direction) create implicit coupling that undermines the separation this zone implies; consider whether these services should be absorbed into the core zone.
- Clean 1:1 source-to-test pairing (2 src files, 2 test files) indicates good test discipline for this subsystem.
- task-usage-tracking has the worst coupling-to-cohesion ratio in this batch (0.38 / 0.63 ≈ 0.60): its external dependencies are nearly as strong as its internal ones, making it the most fragile zone boundary here and the strongest absorption candidate.
- Coupling-to-cohesion ratio of ~0.60 is the highest in this batch — external pull on this zone is nearly as strong as its internal coherence, signaling an unstable boundary.
- usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts imports only from rex-gateway.js and incremental-task-usage.js — no WebSocket import at the source level. The WebSocket broadcast described in its docblock must be injected at the call site rather than imported directly, which means the runtime coupling to websocket-infrastructure is invisible to the static import graph and will not appear in zone coupling scores.
- The scheduler's cleanup cycle cross-references the PRD via rex-gateway, making task-usage-tracking the only satellite zone that directly depends on the gateway module pattern — if the rex-gateway API changes, this zone is an implicit blast-radius target alongside the direct consumers.
- usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts documents that it 'Broadcasts a WebSocket event so the dashboard can react' but does not import websocket infrastructure directly — the WebSocket dependency is injected at the call site, hiding a runtime coupling that the static zone graph cannot measure. This invisible dependency means the zone's coupling score of 0.38 understates its true runtime coupling.
- incremental-task-usage.ts is a domain-layer concern (tracking task events) while usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts is an infrastructure-layer concern (time-driven operational scheduling); mixing two different architectural layers inside a single zone is the structural root cause of the zone's 0.63 cohesion score — not just a lifecycle difference but a layer violation.
- The two files in this zone have different change drivers: incremental-task-usage.ts changes when the domain model changes, usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts changes when operational policy changes. Grouping files with different change drivers in one zone increases the probability that unrelated changes force joint review and test reruns.
- Split task-usage-tracking into two single-responsibility units: a domain zone for incremental-task-usage.ts (task event accumulation) and absorb usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts into web-dashboard-core as an operational infrastructure concern. This resolves the layer violation and eliminates the zone's weak cohesion score.
- [call graph] 100 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 0.99, coupling: 0.01)

</insights>
